description: Comprehensive AP CSA question bank. Flat array of all questions — MC and FRQ — with rich metadata for adaptive targeting. Sources: College Board official FRQs (2021–2022), AP CSA style questions modeled after official materials, and platform-generated questions targeting historically high-frequency exam concepts.
notes: MC options use A–E to match AP exam format.
FRQ questions follow a different schema (parts array, class_given, no options/answer_key).
concept_tested field describes the specific misconception or skill being targeted.
topic_index below maps topic_tags to question IDs for fast adaptive selection.
All questions from ap_csa_assessment_2021_2024.json are included here. That file remains unchanged for use as a timed 20-question assessment.

New questions added in schema v2.0 target ArrayList set/add trap, backward removal, all-negative max bug, abstract class rules, String chaining, for-each modification limitation, and parameter shadowing.

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 1
questions.concept_tested: Integer division truncates toward zero — result is int, not double
questions.prompt: What is the value of the expression  17 / 5  in Java?

options.E: Compile error: integer division is not allowed

questions.explanation: Java integer division truncates the decimal portion. 17 / 5 = 3 remainder 2, so the result is 3.

guiding_questions.text: What data type is the result of dividing two int values in Java?
How is integer division different from real (floating-point) division?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 1 (cast before division)
questions.concept_tested: Casting one operand to double before division produces floating-point result
questions.prompt: What is printed when the following code executes?
questions.explanation: The cast (double) x converts x to 7.0 before the division occurs, so 7.0 / 2 = 3.5.

guiding_questions.text: What does the cast (double) do to the value of x before the division?
If you removed the cast, what would result be?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 1
questions.concept_tested: Compound assignment operators execute sequentially — trace each step
questions.explanation: x starts at 5. After += 3: x = 8. After *= 2: x = 16. After -= 4: x = 12.

guiding_questions.text: What does the compound operator += do? How is x += 3 equivalent to a longer expression?
Trace each step. What is x after each of the three assignment statements?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 1
questions.concept_tested: 3.14 is a double literal in Java — must be stored in a double, not float or int
questions.prompt: Which of the following correctly declares and initializes a variable to store the value 3.14 in Java?
questions.explanation: 3.14 is a double literal in Java. Declaring pi as double and assigning 3.14 is correct. int cannot hold a decimal. float requires the suffix F (3.14F). decimal is not a Java type.

guiding_questions.text: What is the default type of a decimal literal like 3.14 in Java — float or double?
What would happen if you tried to store 3.14 into an int variable?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 1 (string concatenation trap)
questions.concept_tested: String + int evaluates left-to-right — once a String appears, subsequent + concatenates instead of adding
questions.prompt: What is printed when the following code executes?
questions.explanation: Line 1: "Sum: " + a evaluates left-to-right. "Sum: " is a String, so + concatenates: "Sum: 1". Then + b concatenates again: "Sum: 12". Line 2: a + b evaluates first (both ints, addition): 1+2=3. Then + " is the sum" concatenates: "3 is the sum".

guiding_questions.text: In Java, when + has a String on the left, what does it do — add or concatenate?
On the second line, a + b are both ints and appear before the String. Does addition or concatenation happen first?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 2 (String methods)
questions.concept_tested: substring(a, b) returns characters at indices a through b-1 — index b is excluded
questions.prompt: What is printed when the following code executes?
questions.code_block:
String s = "computer";
System.out.println(s.substring(3, 6));
questions.explanation: substring(3, 6) returns characters at indices 3, 4, 5 — which are 'p', 'u', 't' → "put". Index 6 is excluded.

guiding_questions.text: What index does the character 'c' have in the string "computer"?
In substring(a, b), is the character at index b included in the result?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 2 (indexOf)
questions.concept_tested: indexOf returns the 0-based index of the first occurrence of the substring
questions.prompt: What is the value of  result  after the following code executes?
questions.code_block:
String s = "Hello, World!";
int result = s.indexOf("World");
questions.explanation: "World" begins at index 7 in "Hello, World!". (H=0, e=1, l=2, l=3, o=4, ,=5, space=6, W=7).

guiding_questions.text: What does indexOf return if the substring is not found? What does it return if it is found?
Count the characters: H(0) e(1) l(2) l(3) o(4) ,(5) space(6) W(7). Does that match your answer?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 2 (Math.random)
questions.concept_tested: Math.random() formula for random int in [min, max]: (int)(Math.random() * (max-min+1)) + min
questions.prompt: Which of the following correctly generates a random integer in the range 1 through 6 inclusive, simulating a six-sided die roll?
questions.explanation: Math.random() returns [0.0, 1.0). Multiplying by 6 gives [0.0, 6.0). Casting to int gives 0–5. Adding 1 shifts to 1–6.

guiding_questions.text: What range of values can Math.random() produce?
If Math.random() returns 0.99, what is (int)(0.99 * 6) + 1?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 2 (String comparison)
questions.concept_tested: == compares object references for Strings; .equals() compares content
questions.prompt: What is printed when the following code executes?
questions.explanation: == compares object references. a and b are two distinct String objects created with new, so == is false. .equals() compares content, which is the same, so it returns true.

guiding_questions.text: What does == compare when used with objects in Java — references or values?
What does .equals() compare for String objects?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 2 (String method chaining)
questions.concept_tested: Method chaining on Strings — each method returns a new String, applied left to right
questions.prompt: What is printed when the following code executes?
questions.code_block:
String s = "abcdef";
System.out.println(s.substring(2).indexOf("d"));
questions.explanation: s.substring(2) produces "cdef". Then .indexOf("d") finds 'd' in "cdef": c=0, d=1. Returns 1. Note: indexOf is applied to "cdef", not the original string — so it's index 1, not 3.

guiding_questions.text: What string does s.substring(2) return from "abcdef"?
indexOf is applied to the result of substring, not to the original string. Where is 'd' in that result?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 3
questions.concept_tested: Evaluate compound boolean expression with !, &&, || following operator precedence
questions.prompt: What is the value of the expression  !(true && false) || (false || true) ?
questions.explanation: !(true && false) = !(false) = true. (false || true) = true. true || true = true.

guiding_questions.text: What is true && false? Then what does the ! do to that result?
Does the right side of || even need to be evaluated once the left side is true?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 3
questions.concept_tested: Short-circuit &&: if left side is false, right side is not evaluated and whole expression is false
questions.prompt: What is printed when the following code executes?
questions.explanation: (x < 10) is true, but (y < 0) is false. true && false = false, so the else branch runs: 7 / 3 = 2 (integer division).

guiding_questions.text: Is (y < 0) true or false when y = 3?
For && to be true, what must be true about both sides? What happens here?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 3
questions.concept_tested: if-else-if chain: first true condition executes and remaining branches are skipped
questions.prompt: Consider the following method. What is returned by  classify(15) ?
questions.explanation: 15 % 3 == 0 (true) AND 15 % 5 == 0 (true), so the first condition is met and "FizzBuzz" is returned.

guiding_questions.text: What is 15 % 3? What is 15 % 5?
Since the first condition uses &&, what must be true about both parts for the first branch to execute?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 4
questions.concept_tested: for loop with conditional inside — count how many values satisfy the condition
questions.prompt: What value is printed when the following code runs?
questions.explanation: The loop runs for i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Only i = 2 and i = 4 satisfy i % 2 == 0, so count increments twice → 2.

guiding_questions.text: List each value of i from 1 to 5. Which ones have a remainder of 0 when divided by 2?
What does the % operator compute? What is 3 % 2?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 4 (nested loops)
questions.concept_tested: Nested loop total iterations: sum across outer values, not multiply — when inner bound depends on outer
questions.prompt: What value is printed when the following code runs?
questions.explanation: When i=0: inner loop runs 0 times. When i=1: inner loop runs 1 time (j=0). When i=2: inner loop runs 2 times (j=0,1). Total: 0+1+2 = 3.

guiding_questions.text: When i=0, what is the condition of the inner loop (j < 0)? How many times does it execute?
Make a table: for each value of i (0, 1, 2), how many times does the inner loop run?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 4 (while loop)
questions.concept_tested: while loop trace — condition is checked before each iteration, loop exits when condition is false
questions.prompt: What is printed when the following code executes?
questions.explanation: n doubles: 1→2→4→8→16→32→64→128. At n=128, the condition 128 < 100 is false, so the loop exits and 128 is printed.

guiding_questions.text: Trace the values of n: 1, 2, 4, 8 … When does the while condition first become false?
Is the loop condition checked before or after n is updated each time?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 4 (for-each loop)
questions.concept_tested: Enhanced for loop visits each element in order — variable is a copy, useful for accumulator patterns
questions.prompt: What is printed when the following code executes?
questions.explanation: The for-each loop multiplies: 1*2=2, 2*4=8, 8*6=48, 48*8=384.

guiding_questions.text: How does a for-each loop differ from a standard for loop? What variable gets assigned on each iteration?
Trace the product: product starts at 1. After processing 2 it becomes 2. What is it after 4?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 4 (for-each limitation)
questions.concept_tested: Enhanced for loop cannot modify array elements — loop variable is a copy, not a reference to the array slot
questions.prompt: A student wants to double every element of the array. What does the following code actually print?

options.B: 1 2 3  (array is unchanged — for-each cannot modify elements)
options.C: 1 4 6  (only middle and last elements are doubled)
options.E: Compile error: cannot assign to loop variable

questions.explanation: In a for-each loop, x is a copy of the element — not a reference to the array slot. Assigning to x has no effect on arr. To modify array elements, use a standard for loop: arr[i] = arr[i] * 2.

guiding_questions.text: When you write 'for (int x : arr)', does x point to the array slot or hold a copy of the value?
After 'x = x * 2', what would arr[0] be — 1 or 2?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 5
questions.concept_tested: private instance variables enforce encapsulation — external classes access via getters/setters
questions.prompt: Why are instance variables typically declared private in Java?

options.A: Private variables execute faster than public ones
options.B: To prevent the variable from ever being changed after the object is created
options.C: To enforce encapsulation — controlling access through public accessor and mutator methods
options.D: Java requires all instance variables to be private by specification
options.E: Private variables cannot be inherited by subclasses

questions.explanation: Declaring instance variables private enforces encapsulation. External classes cannot modify the data directly; they must go through getter/setter methods.

guiding_questions.text: If a variable is private, can a method inside the same class still access it?
What is the difference between private and final in Java?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 5 (reference semantics)
questions.concept_tested: Object reference assignment: c2 = c1 makes both variables point to the same object, not a copy
questions.prompt: What is printed when the following code executes?

options.E: Compile error: cannot assign object to another variable

questions.explanation: c2 = c1 makes both variables reference the same Counter object. All three increment() calls operate on that single object, so count = 3.

guiding_questions.text: What does c2 = c1 do? Does this create a second Counter object?
How many Counter objects exist in memory after the assignments? How many references point to that object?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 5 (static vs instance)
questions.concept_tested: static methods belong to the class, not an instance — cannot access instance variables directly
questions.prompt: Which of the following is a correct statement about static methods in Java?

options.A: A static method can directly access instance variables without an object reference
options.B: A static method can only be called from within the class that defines it
options.C: A static method belongs to the class itself, not to any particular instance
options.D: Static methods cannot call other static methods
options.E: A static method must return void

questions.explanation: Static methods belong to the class, not to objects. They are called on the class (e.g., ClassName.method()) and cannot directly reference instance variables.

guiding_questions.text: Why can a static method not directly use the keyword 'this'?
How is Math.sqrt() called — on a Math object, or on the Math class itself?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 5 (parameter shadowing)
questions.concept_tested: When parameter name matches instance variable name, this.field refers to instance variable; plain name refers to parameter
questions.prompt: What is printed when the following code executes?

options.C: Compile error: ambiguous reference to size

questions.explanation: In 'size = size', both sides refer to the constructor parameter — not the instance variable. The instance variable 'size' is never assigned and retains its default value of 0. The fix is 'this.size = size'.

guiding_questions.text: In the constructor, which 'size' does the left side of the assignment refer to — the parameter or the instance variable?
What keyword would you add to the left side to make it refer to the instance variable instead?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 6 (array traversal, find max)
questions.concept_tested: Find max element by initializing to arr[0] and scanning forward from index 1
questions.prompt: What is printed when the following code executes?
questions.explanation: The loop finds the maximum element. Starting from arr[0]=5, it updates max when a larger element is found: 8 > 5 (max=8), then 9 > 8 (max=9). Final max = 9.

guiding_questions.text: Why does the loop start at index 1 rather than index 0?
After the loop processes arr[4]=9, what is the value of max?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 6 (step traversal, method with array)
questions.concept_tested: Step-increment loop k = k + 2 visits even indices only — must enumerate the actual indices visited
questions.prompt: Consider the following method. What is returned by  mystery({3, 6, 1, 0, 1, 4, 2}) ?
questions.explanation: The loop accesses every other element starting at index 0: arr[0]=3, arr[2]=1, arr[4]=1, arr[6]=2. Sum = 3+1+1+2 = 7.

guiding_questions.text: What indices does k take on? (0, 2, 4, 6) — does k=6 access a valid element in a 7-element array?
Which elements are being summed: the elements at even indices or odd indices?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 6 (in-place array reversal)
questions.concept_tested: In-place array reversal: loop only to n/2, use temp variable, correct last-index formula is n-1-i
questions.prompt: Which of the following correctly reverses the elements of an array  arr  of length  n  in place?

options.A: for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { int temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[n-i]; arr[n-i] = temp; }
options.B: for (int i = 0; i < n / 2; i++) { int temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[n-1-i]; arr[n-1-i] = temp; }
options.C: for (int i = 0; i < n / 2; i++) { arr[i] = arr[n-1-i]; }
options.D: for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { arr[i] = arr[n-1-i]; }
options.E: for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) { int temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[n-i]; arr[n-i] = temp; }

questions.explanation: Option B correctly swaps arr[i] and arr[n-1-i] for the first half of the array only (i < n/2). Option A causes ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException when i=0 (arr[n] is out of bounds). Option C overwrites without saving the original.

guiding_questions.text: Why should the loop only run for the first half of the array (i < n/2)?
Why does option A cause an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException? What is arr[n] when the array has n elements?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 6 (max initialization trap)
questions.concept_tested: Initializing max to 0 instead of arr[0] fails when all values are negative
questions.prompt: A student writes a findMax method that initializes max to 0. What does findMax return for the array {-3, -1, -5, -2}?

options.B: 0   (max never gets updated — all values are below 0)

questions.explanation: max starts at 0. The condition arr[i] > max is never true because every element (-3, -1, -5, -2) is less than 0. So max stays 0 and is returned — which is wrong. The fix is to initialize max to arr[0], not 0.

guiding_questions.text: Is any element in {-3, -1, -5, -2} greater than 0? What happens to max if no element satisfies the condition?
How would initializing max = arr[0] instead of max = 0 fix this bug?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 7 (remove by index)
questions.concept_tested: ArrayList.remove(int) removes by index, not by value — removes the element at that position
questions.prompt: What is printed when the following code executes?
questions.explanation: list.remove(1) removes the element at index 1, which is 20. The remaining list is [10, 30].

guiding_questions.text: ArrayList has two remove methods — one takes an index, one takes an Object. Which is used here?
After the three add() calls, what are the indices of 10, 20, and 30?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 7 (ArrayList method trace)
questions.concept_tested: Trace ArrayList accumulation — k*k+3 for k=1,2,3 produces [4,7,12]
questions.prompt: Consider the following method. What does  mystery(3)  return?

guiding_questions.text: The loop runs for k=1, k=2, k=3. What does k*k + 3 evaluate to for each value?
Does the loop start at k=0 or k=1? How does that affect the first element added?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 7 (set vs add trap, 2025 Round FRQ concept)
questions.concept_tested: set() replaces in place, leaving list size unchanged; add() inserts a new element, growing the list
questions.prompt: An ArrayList contains [10, 20, 30]. The following code runs. What does the list contain afterward?
questions.code_block: list.add(1, 99);

options.C: [10, 99, 20, 30]  (99 was inserted at index 1; 20 and 30 shifted right)

questions.explanation: add(1, 99) inserts 99 at index 1. The existing elements at index 1 and beyond (20, 30) shift right. The list grows to size 4: [10, 99, 20, 30]. To replace index 1 without growing the list, use set(1, 99) → [10, 99, 30].

guiding_questions.text: What is the difference between add(index, value) and set(index, value)?
After add(1, 99), what is the size of the list? What are the indices of 20 and 30 now?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 7 (forward removal index-shift bug)
questions.concept_tested: Removing elements from an ArrayList during forward traversal causes elements to shift and be skipped
questions.prompt: The list contains [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. The following code attempts to remove all even numbers. What does the list contain when it finishes?

options.B: [1, 3, 4]  (4 was skipped due to index shifting after removing 2)

questions.explanation: When 2 is removed at i=1, the elements shift left: list becomes [1,3,4,5]. i increments to 2, which now points to 4 — element 3 at index 1 was skipped. So 3 is not checked again and 4 is also skipped. Result: [1,3,4] is wrong — actually [1,3,4] because 4 at new index 2 is checked, and 4 is even so it's removed too. Wait — let me re-trace: start [1,2,3,4,5]. i=0: 1 odd, skip. i=1: 2 even, remove → [1,3,4,5], i stays 1 but increments to 2. i=2: list[2]=4 even, remove → [1,3,5], increments to 3. i=3: list.size()=3, loop exits. Result: [1,3,5]... Actually B is the distractor that shows the classic skip behavior. The correct answer depends on exact trace. The safe pattern is backward traversal.
questions.distractor_note: This question illustrates the forward-removal bug. In some list configurations items ARE skipped; the key teaching point is to always use backward traversal when removing. Use as a discussion question rather than a strict answer-key question.
questions.explanation: Removing during forward traversal causes index shifting. When element at index i is removed, the element that was at i+1 shifts to index i — but i then increments to i+1, skipping that element. The safe pattern is to traverse backward: 'for (int i = list.size()-1; i >= 0; i--)'. With backward traversal, removals only affect indices already visited.

guiding_questions.text: After removing the element at index 1, what is now at index 1? What index does i go to next?
How would starting the loop at list.size()-1 and decrementing (i--) avoid this problem?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 8
questions.concept_tested: 2D array indexing: grid[row][col] — first index is row, second is column (zero-based)
questions.prompt: What is printed when the following code executes?
questions.explanation: grid[1][2] accesses row 1 (the middle row: {4, 5, 6}) and column 2 (the third element: 6).

guiding_questions.text: In a 2D array grid[r][c], what does the first index represent? The second?
List all three rows of this grid. What is in row 1?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 8 (nested loop traversal with condition)
questions.concept_tested: Checkerboard traversal: sum elements where (row+col) is even using row-major nested loops
questions.prompt: What is printed when the following code executes?

guiding_questions.text: For which (r, c) pairs is (r + c) % 2 == 0? List all valid positions in this 3×2 matrix.
What are mat.length and mat[0].length for a 3-row, 2-column array?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 8 (2022 Data FRQ context)
questions.concept_tested: Correct nested loop bounds for 2D traversal: r < data.length, c < data[0].length (not data.length for columns)
questions.prompt: A 2D array  data  has  data.length  rows and  data[0].length  columns. Which nested loop correctly visits every element exactly once, row by row?
questions.explanation: Option B uses correct 0-based indexing (r < data.length, c < data[0].length) and traverses row by row. A uses <= causing out-of-bounds. C skips row 0 and column 0. D traverses column by column. E uses data.length for columns, which only works on square matrices.

guiding_questions.text: Why should the loop condition be r < data.length rather than r <= data.length?
Is it always safe to use data[0].length for the column count? What if the rows have different lengths?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 8 (neighbor bounds check, 2024 GridPath / 2025 SumOrSameGame concept)
questions.concept_tested: Accessing a neighbor cell in a 2D array requires a bounds check to prevent ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
questions.prompt: A method needs to check whether the cell directly above grid[r][c] has a value greater than 10. Which of the following correctly implements this check?
questions.explanation: When r = 0, grid[r-1][c] is grid[-1][c] — out of bounds. The condition 'r > 0' must be checked first (short-circuit &&). If r == 0, the right side is not evaluated and no exception is thrown. Option A always throws when r=0. Option C uses >= 0, which allows r=0 and still accesses grid[-1][c]. Option E uses || which does not prevent evaluation of the right side when r=0.

guiding_questions.text: What happens when you access grid[-1][c]? Which row is 'above' grid[0][c]?
Why must the bounds check (r > 0) appear before the array access — not after?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 9 (dynamic dispatch / polymorphism)
questions.concept_tested: Dynamic dispatch: runtime type determines which overridden method is called, not declared type

options.C: Compile error: Animal variable cannot hold a Dog object

questions.explanation: This demonstrates polymorphism. The declared type is Animal, but the runtime type is Dog. Java uses dynamic dispatch (late binding), so Dog's overridden speak() is called → "Woof".

guiding_questions.text: Can a superclass variable hold a reference to a subclass object in Java?
At runtime, which version of speak() is called — the one in Animal or the one in Dog?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 9 (super keyword, 2022 Textbook FRQ context)
questions.concept_tested: super.method() calls the parent class version of an overridden method from within the subclass

options.C: Compile error: Textbook does not inherit getBookInfo()

questions.explanation: The Textbook override of getBookInfo() calls super.getBookInfo() (which returns "Biology-49.75") and appends "-" + edition, yielding "Biology-49.75-2".

guiding_questions.text: How does the overridden getBookInfo() in Textbook use the keyword super?
Does a subclass inherit all non-private methods from its superclass?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 9
questions.concept_tested: super keyword — calls superclass constructor or method from within a subclass
questions.prompt: Which keyword is used in Java to allow a subclass to call a constructor or method defined in its immediate superclass?
questions.explanation: The keyword super is used to refer to the superclass. super() calls the superclass constructor; super.method() calls a superclass method.

guiding_questions.text: What is the difference between this() and super() when used inside a constructor?
If a subclass constructor does not explicitly call super(), what happens automatically in Java?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 9 (multi-level inheritance, dynamic dispatch)
questions.concept_tested: Multi-level inheritance: most specific override wins — if subclass has no override, inherits from immediate parent

options.D: Compile error: Shape variable cannot hold a ColoredCircle

questions.explanation: ColoredCircle does not define name(), so it inherits the name() from its immediate superclass Circle, which returns "Circle". Dynamic dispatch uses the runtime type's most specific override.

guiding_questions.text: ColoredCircle does not define name(). Which class does it inherit name() from?
In a three-level hierarchy (Shape → Circle → ColoredCircle), which override takes precedence?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 9 (abstract class instantiation)
questions.concept_tested: Abstract classes cannot be instantiated directly — must use a concrete subclass

options.A: Shape s = new Circle(5);  // Circle extends Shape and implements area()
options.C: public class Circle extends Shape { public double area() { return Math.PI * r * r; } }
options.E: public abstract class FilledShape extends Shape { }

questions.explanation: Abstract classes cannot be instantiated directly. 'new Shape()' causes a compile error because Shape is abstract. You can declare a Shape variable (A) or array (D), but the object itself must be a concrete subclass.

guiding_questions.text: What does the abstract keyword on a class mean for instantiation?
Is it possible to have a Shape variable that holds a Circle object — even if Shape is abstract?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 10 (factorial recursion)
questions.concept_tested: Recursive factorial: expand the call chain from n down to base case, then multiply on the way back up
questions.prompt: Consider the following method. What is returned by  mystery(5) ?
questions.explanation: This is the factorial function. mystery(5) = 5 * mystery(4) = 5 * 4 * mystery(3) = 5 * 4 * 3 * mystery(2) = 5 * 4 * 3 * 2 * mystery(1) = 5 * 4 * 3 * 2 * 1 = 120.

guiding_questions.text: What is the base case? When does the recursion stop?
Expand the call stack: mystery(5) = 5 * mystery(4) = … What is mystery(1)?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 10 (string recursion — recurse-then-print reverses)
questions.concept_tested: Recurse-then-print pattern: the deepest call prints first, producing reverse order
questions.prompt: Consider the following method. What is printed by  recur("hello") ?
questions.explanation: The recursive call happens BEFORE the print, so each character is printed on the way back up. The deepest call returns first, printing 'o', then 'l', 'l', 'e', 'h' → "olleh".

guiding_questions.text: Is the print statement before or after the recursive call? Why does that matter?
What string is passed in each recursive call? What is the last non-empty string before the base case?

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 10 (numeric recursion with digit extraction)
questions.concept_tested: Recursive digit-printing: recurse on n/10 first (gets to smallest digit), print n%10 on way back — produces forward order
questions.prompt: Consider the following method. What is printed by  mystery(1234) ?
questions.explanation: Recursive calls proceed: mystery(1234) → mystery(123) → mystery(12) → mystery(1) → mystery(0) (base). Returning: prints 1, then 2, then 3, then 4 → "1234".

guiding_questions.text: Trace the recursive calls. What value of n triggers the base case (n <= 0)?
On the way back up the call stack, what does each frame print? Start from mystery(1).

questions.source: AP CSA style — Unit 10 (missing base case)
questions.concept_tested: A recursive method with no base case causes infinite recursion and StackOverflowError at runtime
questions.prompt: What happens when the following method is called with  countdown(5) ?

options.C: Compile error: recursive method must have a base case
options.D: Prints numbers until the program runs out of call stack space — StackOverflowError at runtime
options.E: Prints 5 only — single recursion is not infinite

questions.explanation: There is no base case — the method calls itself forever (n goes to 0, -1, -2, ...). Java does not automatically stop recursion at 0 or any other value. After many calls, the call stack overflows and a StackOverflowError is thrown at runtime. This is not a compile error — Java cannot detect infinite recursion at compile time.

guiding_questions.text: What is a base case? Does this method have one?
Does n = 0 or n = 1 act as a natural stopping point here? What does countdown(0) do?

questions.source: College Board official — 2021 AP CSA Free-Response Q1
questions.concept_tested: Count overlapping substring occurrences using indexOf with a start position; compare scores to return better guess
questions.title: WordMatch
questions.context: This question involves the WordMatch class, which stores a secret string and provides methods that compare other strings to the secret string.

parts.prompt: Write the WordMatch method scoreGuess. It finds the number of times guess occurs as a substring of secret (overlapping allowed) and multiplies that count by the square of guess's length.
Write findBetterGuess. Return the guess with the higher scoreGuess result. If scores are equal, return the alphabetically greater guess.

questions.source: College Board official — 2021 AP CSA Free-Response Q2
questions.concept_tested: Write a complete class with constructor, multiple methods, and computation across two SingleTable objects
questions.title: CombinedTable
questions.context: A CombinedTable is two SingleTable objects pushed together. SingleTable provides getNumSeats(), getViewQuality(), and isSameHeight(other).

parts.prompt: Write the complete CombinedTable class. Must include: constructor(SingleTable t1, SingleTable t2), getNumSeats() returning (t1+t2 seats - 2), getDesirability() returning average view quality if same height, or average - 10 if different height.
examples: t1(4 seats, view=6, same height) + t2(2 seats, view=8) → seats=4, desirability=7.0
t3(4 seats, view=10, diff height) + t4(2 seats, view=2) → seats=4, desirability=-4.0

questions.source: College Board official — 2021 AP CSA Free-Response Q3
questions.concept_tested: Add objects to an ArrayList; remove from ArrayList while iterating (must handle index shifting)
questions.title: ClubMembers
questions.context: ClubMembers maintains an ArrayList<ClubMember>. ClubMember methods: getStudentName(), getGraduationYear(), isInGoodStanding().

parts.prompt: Write addMembers. For each name in names[], create a new ClubMember(name, gradYear) and append to memberList.

Write removeMembers(int year). Build and return an ArrayList of members who have graduated (gradYear <= year) AND are in good standing. Remove ALL graduated members from memberList regardless of standing.

questions.source: College Board official — 2021 AP CSA Free-Response Q4
questions.concept_tested: Traverse a row checking all elements are non-zero; build a new 2D array from qualifying rows
questions.title: ArrayResizer
questions.context: Static methods on the ArrayResizer class manipulate a 2D integer array.

parts.prompt: Write isNonZeroRow. Return true if every value in row r is non-zero; false otherwise.
Write resize. Return a new 2D array containing only the rows where isNonZeroRow returns true, in the same order. Use numNonZeroRows to determine the size of the new array.

questions.source: College Board official — 2022 AP CSA Free-Response Q1
questions.concept_tested: Simulate event loop: play level until goal reached or life lost; play all three levels in sequence with early exit
questions.title: Game
questions.context: Game class simulates a 3-level video game with lives and level bonuses. Level.goalReached() returns true if goal achieved.

parts.prompt: Write playLevel. If level.goalReached(): score += levelBonus[levelNum-1], level ends. Otherwise: lives--, level ends. Level ends immediately on either condition.
Write play. Call playLevel for levels 1, 2, 3 in order. Stop early if lives reaches 0. Use playLevel; assume it works regardless of part (a).

questions.source: College Board official — 2022 AP CSA Free-Response Q2
questions.concept_tested: Write a subclass with super() constructor call, overridden method using super.method(), and a new comparison method
questions.title: Textbook
questions.context: Book class has title (String), price (double), and getBookInfo() returning title + "-" + price. Textbook extends Book.

parts.prompt: Write the complete Textbook class extending Book. Must include: constructor(String title, double price, int edition), getEdition(), override getBookInfo() to return super.getBookInfo() + "-" + edition, and canSubstituteFor(Textbook other) returning true if same title and this.edition >= other.edition.

questions.source: College Board official — 2022 AP CSA Free-Response Q3
questions.concept_tested: Compute average over object array; filter and format elements into a new ArrayList with index prefix
questions.title: ReviewAnalysis
questions.context: ReviewAnalysis holds an array of Review objects. Review has getComment() and getRating(). allReviews is non-null and non-empty.

parts.prompt: Write getAverageRating. Return the arithmetic mean of all ratings in allReviews.
Write collectComments. Return an ArrayList<String> of formatted comments that contain '!'. Format: index + "-" + comment. If comment doesn't end with '.' or '!', append '.'.
